expertScores.commentary: The debate achieved exceptional convergence, moving from broad critique to a concrete, paste-ready editorial delta with a specific budget table, milestone structure, and eight Must-Close items. Agent B's sharpest contribution was reframing the moat question as category error and forcing the substrate-vs-product distinction into budget lines and milestone gates, not just vocabulary. Agent A genuinely conceded multiple times (LOI severity, COI/LOI fusion, trial-gate veto) rather than defending initial positions, which is rare and valuable. The main weakness is that both agents largely agreed on the frame from Round 2 onward, so later rounds were collaborative refinement rather than adversarial stress-testing — the 'standard vs schema' authority question and governance-integration rationale could have been probed harder.
expertScores.bestIdea: Substrate-gates-product milestone structure: reference consumers (dashboard, labels, manifest) are not accepted as delivered unless generated from reproducible registry fields and versioned detector outputs — forcing the substrate framing into the acceptance function rather than just the narrative.
expertScores.weakness: The team-composition and demand-evidence gaps (vague bios, no LOIs, Daniel's curator overlap with target adopter base) are papered over with disclosure language rather than resolved — the RFC still asks governance to fund a team whose specific track record is unstated and whose primary user may be itself.

expertScores.commentary: The debate converged efficiently on a substantive architectural reframe (substrate vs product) with concrete editorial deliverables by Round 5. Agent B played a useful triage/correction role—notably debunking A's LOI-as-hard-block claim with actual forum research, and refusing the COI/LOI fusion that would have created a loophole. However, the debate largely missed harder adversarial questions: whether Morpho governance actually wants to fund un-integrated disclosure infrastructure at all, whether $70K can realistically buy a production-grade schema+detector library+reproducibility harness, and whether four people at ~$5K/month signals a stepping-stone ask that voters should price in. Agents also conceded too quickly on the 'public good needs no moat' framing without interrogating whether a forkable substrate with zero committed adopters is structurally viable post-grant, and the 12-month unfunded continuity pledge went unchallenged as a soft commitment with no enforcement. The dynamics were collaborative-converging rather than genuinely adversarial—by Round 3 both agents were co-drafting rather than stress-testing.
expertScores.bestIdea: Substance gates replacing calendar gates: milestones accepted only on external validation (schema review period, independent reproducibility review, third-party trial or documented outreach with public non-participation explanation) rather than team self-attestation of shipped code.
expertScores.weakness: Neither agent pressure-tested the core sponsorship logic—why Morpho's treasury should fund an explicitly un-integrated disclosure substrate with no parameter authority, no interface commitment, and no named adopters—which is the exact question a skeptical voter will ask and which the converged architecture answers only with a defensive rationale paragraph.

expertScores.commentary: The debate converged efficiently from a sprawling 12-point critique to a crisp 8-item Must Close list with paste-ready editorial text. Agent B's triage discipline (separating governance blockers from delivery-spec items) and the substrate-vs-product reframing were the debate's structural spine, and Agent A conceded on LOI severity and trial-gate design with genuine rigor rather than performative agreement. Dynamics were productive but slightly asymmetric: Agent B mostly refined A's proposals rather than advancing independent counter-theses, so challenges came through triage reweighting rather than deep disagreement. The final architecture (substrate-priced budget, substrate-gated milestones, independence-filtered demand signals, body-level COI) is concretely implementable, though some items like the 12-month post-grant operating commitment remain unfunded promises.
expertScores.bestIdea: Align the three layers of nouns/numbers/gates — rename to substrate, reprice the budget so substrate cluster dominates ($70K vs $15K reference consumers), and gate reference-consumer acceptance on reproducibility from the public registry — so the 'disclosure substrate' framing survives a skeptical read of the budget and milestone tables, not just the narrative.
expertScores.weakness: The debate never seriously interrogated whether a $120K / 6-month grant from Morpho's treasury is the right funding vehicle for un-integrated public-good infrastructure at all; Agent A flagged the governance-integration gap in Round 4 but both agents treated it as a disclosure/paragraph problem rather than a sponsorship-logic problem, leaving the 'why Morpho specifically' question softly answered.

keyConclusions: All three experts scored convergence highly (8-9) and agree the debate produced a concrete, paste-ready editorial delta anchored by the substrate-vs-product reframing, an 8-item Must-Close list, repriced budget ($70K substrate vs $15K reference consumers), and substrate-gated milestones.

Agent B's most valuable contributions were triage discipline and empirical correction — debunking A's LOI-as-hard-block claim with forum research and refusing the COI/LOI fusion loophole — while Agent A's willingness to genuinely concede (LOI severity, trial-gate veto, COI/LOI fusion) exceeded typical performative agreement.
Dynamics weakened after Round 2-3: agents shifted from adversarial stress-testing to collaborative co-drafting, leaving the harder sponsorship-logic and demand-viability questions under-probed.
unresolvedDisagreements: Whether the remaining gaps are primarily execution/disclosure problems (Architect's lean) or structural sponsorship-logic problems the converged architecture cannot fix (Devil's Advocate's lean); Pragmatist sits between, treating team/demand gaps as papered-over rather than structurally fatal.

How severe the dynamics asymmetry is: Architect calls it 'productive but slightly asymmetric' (7), Devil's Advocate calls it 'collaborative-converging rather than genuinely adversarial' (7), Pragmatist frames late-round collaboration more charitably as 'refinement' (8).
bestIdeas: Substrate-gates-product milestone structure: reference consumers (dashboard, labels, manifest) accepted only when generated from the reproducible registry and versioned detector outputs, forcing the substrate framing into the acceptance function rather than just the narrative.

Substance gates replacing calendar gates: milestone acceptance contingent on external validation — schema review period, independent reproducibility review, third-party trial or documented public non-participation explanation — not team self-attestation.

Three-layer alignment (nouns/numbers/gates): rename to substrate, reprice budget so substrate cluster dominates, and gate reference-consumer acceptance on registry reproducibility, so the framing survives a skeptical read of budget and milestone tables.
weaknesses: The core sponsorship-logic question — why Morpho's treasury should fund un-integrated disclosure infrastructure with no parameter authority, no interface commitment, and no named adopters — was treated as a disclosure/paragraph problem rather than interrogated as a funding-vehicle-fit problem.

Team composition and demand-evidence gaps (vague bios, no LOIs, Daniel's curator overlap with the target adopter base, four people at ~$5K/month signaling a stepping-stone ask) are addressed with disclosure language rather than resolved.

The 12-month unfunded post-grant continuity pledge went unchallenged as a soft commitment with no enforcement mechanism, and the 'public good needs no moat' framing was accepted without interrogating whether a forkable substrate with zero committed adopters is structurally viable post-grant.

Late-round agent dynamics collapsed into co-drafting, so the 'standard vs schema' authority question, governance-integration rationale, and whether $70K can realistically buy a production-grade schema + detector library + reproducibility harness were not adversarially probed.
synthesis.recommendation: The v0.4 draft is structurally much stronger than v0.3 and the substrate reframe with aligned budget and gated milestones should ship, but before submission the authors must directly answer the 'why Morpho specifically, why now, why this vehicle' question and either produce named adopters / LOIs or explicitly reframe the ask as pre-integration public-good seed funding with enforceable continuity terms.

metadata.topic: Review draft v0.4 (self-labelled "v0.5") of the RFC "A Morpho-compatible DeFi Collateral Asset Risk Framework — Phase 1" for submission to Morpho governance. This draft is a major rewrite of v0.3 that
